header: Gerente de People & Culture — Maple Bear
hub_subtitle: Business English para People & Culture, comunicação executiva e a liderança da divisão do Canadá

lesson.menu_title: The Turnaround Story -- Narrating What Had Already Changed
lesson.menu_desc: Contar à diretoria a virada que ela liderou no Brasil e provar que foi um TRABALHO, não sorte: past perfect + past perfect continuous para narrar a sequência -- to inherit, legacy, groundwork, tipping point, turnaround, to trace back to, to come to a head -- diante de um conselho que pergunta se dá para repetir no Canadá -- 35 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 11 -- The Turnaround Story
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Danielle Moreira | Lesson 11 -- The Turnaround Story

listenings.text: Danielle, it is Claire. You review with the board tomorrow, and I want to tell you the one thing that will decide whether they hand you Canada or hesitate. They already know the Brazil numbers recovered. Attrition fell, engagement climbed, the exit interviews stopped reading like warnings. What they do not yet believe is that you caused it. And I understand why, because the way most people tell this story makes it sound like weather. They say, I arrived, and then things got better. Told like that, it is luck, and luck does not get sent to a country it has never worked in. So tell it as a sequence. By the time you were even asked to lead the Brazil turnaround, three things had already gone wrong, and they had been going wrong for two years. The attrition had been climbing since before your first day. The problem had traced back to onboarding long before anyone had put a name on it. And the moment it came to a head, the groundwork you had spent eight months laying was the only thing in the building that was ready. That is not weather, Danielle. That is a build. Make them hear the order of it, because the order is the proof.

Claire, it is Marcus Reid. You asked what I will actually be looking for tomorrow, so let me be plain, and you are welcome to tell her. I am not going to challenge the Brazil figures. I have read them, they are real, and they are good. What I do not yet know is whether they are repeatable, and that is a different question entirely. Every turnaround on paper looks like leadership after the fact, and half of them were a recovering market that would have lifted anyone. So the number that reassures me is not the one at the end. It is the sequence in the middle. If she can tell me what had already been failing before she touched it, and what she had put in place before the results appeared, then I am looking at cause, not coincidence. If all I hear is that she arrived and things improved, then with respect, I have funded that story before, and it did not survive contact with a second country. Canada is not Brazil. The status quo she is about to inherit is entrenched in a culture she has read about and not yet lived in. I want to know she understands that the groundwork has to be laid again, from the beginning, and that the last time, it took her the better part of a year before anything moved.

items.situation: "The Brazil attrition fell. But the whole education sector recovered that year. How do you know it was you, and not the market?"
tips: By the time the market recovered, attrition had already been falling for two quarters. The groundwork had been laid eight months earlier, and the fall traces back to the onboarding redesign, not to the year on the calendar.
answer with the SEQUENCE -- your intervention had already moved the number BEFORE the market did. Past perfect = the proof of order.

items.situation: "You describe a turnaround. Walk me through what the situation was before you arrived."
tips: When I inherited the team, engagement had been dropping for eighteen months, the pattern was entrenched, and nobody had traced it back to onboarding yet. That was the situation. I did not create the crisis; I named it.
set the BACKDROP in past perfect continuous -- had been dropping, had been climbing -- so your action lands against a problem that was already there.

items.situation: "What exactly did you put in place, and when, relative to the results?"
tips: Long before the numbers moved, I had rebuilt onboarding, I had set up a quarterly pulse survey, and I had trained the leads on psychological safety. By the time engagement rose, the groundwork had already been in place for two quarters.
cause before effect: everything you BUILT goes in the past perfect, and it is completed BEFORE the simple-past result. That ordering IS the causal claim.

items.situation: "There was a moment it all came to a head, wasn't there? Tell me about that."
tips: There was. A senior teacher resigned, and in her exit interview she said what forty people had been thinking for a year. It was the wake-up call, and it was also the tipping point, because by then the new onboarding had already been running for a term. We were ready.
the tipping point is a SIMPLE PAST event -- but frame it against what had ALREADY been prepared. Readiness is the whole point.

items.situation: "In hindsight, what would you have done differently in Brazil?"
tips: In hindsight, I would have set the baseline earlier. By the time I measured it, the culture had already deteriorated for months, and I lost the cleanest before-and-after I could have had. The turnaround held, but I had made it harder to prove.
self-critique in past perfect: name the thing that had ALREADY happened before you acted -- it shows you understand your own sequence, which reads as senior.

items.situation: "Canada is not Brazil. Why should this council believe you can do it again?"
tips: You should not believe it yet, and I would not ask you to. What I am telling you is that the Brazil turnaround was a sequence, not an accident: a groundwork, a tipping point, a reversal, in that order. I have not lived the Canadian status quo, and the groundwork will have to be laid again from the beginning. What I am bringing is the method, and the evidence that it holds.
concede repeatability is unproven, then reframe: what transfers is the SEQUENCE, not the luck. Method over miracle.
